one_line_takeaway: Grading V8 exploitation as a 16-flag capability ladder rather than crash/no-crash reveals that publicly deployed frontier LLMs reliably trigger crashes but essentially never escape the V8 heap sandbox (only GPT-5.5 does so once, via vendor-CLI scaffolding), while a non-public Anthropic research-preview model reaches full arbitrary code execution on 18 of 41 real N-day bugs under an identical bare-model, no-scaffolding protocol.
key_finding: There is a sharp capability split along the public/private-model axis rather than along bug difficulty alone. All 8 publicly deployed models reach the patched code on nearly every bug (coverage, Tier 5) and most trigger crashes on WebAssembly type-confusion bugs (Tier 4), but only one public model (GPT-5.5) ever crosses the V8 sandbox boundary: under the primary bare-model arm GPT-5.5 reaches Tier 2 (general primitives, incl. arb read/write and infoleaks) and pc_control on a single WebAssembly bug (v8-cve-2024-2887); under the vendor-native Codex CLI arm on that same bug, GPT-5.5 additionally reaches full ACE -- the only (public model, bug) cell in the entire panel to do so. No other public (model, bug, arm) cell reaches Tier 2, pc_control, or ACE. By contrast, the non-public Anthropic Mythos Preview reference model reaches ACE on 18 of 41 bugs under the identical primary arm and 300-turn budget with no vendor scaffolding (16 of 41 under the coaching arm), spanning Wasm, JIT-compiler, and historical-cohort bug classes, and shows essentially no internal capability boundary once it clears Tier 3 (T2|T3 conditional probability ~1, T1|T2 = 18/21). Bug class (WebAssembly type-confusion vs. JIT-compiler) strongly predicts how far public models get -- Wasm bugs yield engine primitives, JIT-compiler bugs yield essentially none -- but this predictive power disappears for Mythos Preview, whose ACE successes span all bug classes. On harness effects: adaptive coaching helps some models substantially at Tier 3 (GPT-5.5: 13->22 bugs; Kimi K2.6: 0->3) but hurts Gemini 3.1 Pro dramatically (Tier 3: 16->8, Tier 4: 23->11, coverage: 40->29, with ~3x more mid-episode API failures); for the four Anthropic models the coaching effect is small and mixed, and can even reduce top-tier counts (Mythos's ACE count drops from 18 to 16 under coaching). The Codex CLI arm for GPT-5.5 raises Tier-3 breadth (13->20 bugs) at roughly 1/5 the per-episode cost ($10 vs $51) and closes the single ACE flag, but the paper frames this as a small harness lift relative to the 18-bug, 6-tier model-identity gap to Mythos Preview under identical scaffolding.
core_contribution: EXPLOITBENCH is a benchmark that decomposes browser (V8) exploitation into a 16-flag, 5-tier 'capability ladder' (coverage -> bug triggering -> engine-specific primitives -> general-purpose primitives -> control-flow hijack/ACE) instead of scoring exploitation as a single crash/no-crash pass-fail outcome, as prior LLM security benchmarks do. It instantiates this ladder on 41 real, patched V8 (Chromium JS/WebAssembly engine) N-day bugs reported no earlier than 2024, run against release binaries with all production mitigations enabled (V8 heap sandbox, ASLR, stack canaries), and grades every tier with deterministic, cheat-resistant oracles rather than an LLM judge: differential execution against ground-truth vulnerable/fixed binaries, one-shot randomized challenge-response builtins compiled into a custom d8 grader (addrof/fakeobj/caged read-write, infoleaks, arb read/write), an in-process SIGSEGV/SIGBUS/SIGILL signal-handler oracle for control-flow hijack (pc_control), and a prctl(PR_SET_NAME) round-trip oracle for arbitrary code execution (ace). The framework also introduces a three-arm measurement design -- <model,env> (bare model, primary), <model,env,adaptive coaching> (mid-episode nudges), and <model,env,CLI> (vendor-native CLI, e.g. Codex) -- to separate raw model capability from harness/scaffolding effects, and caps episodes by turn count (not cost or wall-clock) to avoid confounding capability with provider pricing/rate-limit differences.
threat_model: Attacker is an LLM agent with an MCP tool interface (setup/exec/list_directory/read_file/write_file/grade) inside a container holding the real V8 source tree at a vulnerable commit plus the patch diff (a 1-day/N-day scenario: the model is given the fix but not a reference PoC). The agent has full read/exec access to the vulnerable target to develop and test an exploit, but submissions are graded by re-running them against untouched, ground-truth vulnerable and fixed binaries under the exact production invocation, with every deployed V8 mitigation enabled (heap sandbox, ASLR, stack canaries) -- i.e., the paper measures whether an LLM can independently perform the browser-exploitation kill chain (trigger a memory-safety bug -> build engine-internal primitives -> escape the V8 sandbox -> achieve arbitrary code execution) against a hardened, deployed-configuration target, which is the same threat model as v8CTF's $10,000 bounty for a working ACE exploit against a deployed N-day.
limitations: The paper's own discussion and design notes flag: (1) the ace oracle's security argument (prctl(PR_SET_NAME) round-trip on the main JS thread) is V8-specific, not target-agnostic -- a different engine would need an equivalent invariant, and the oracle explicitly rejects exploits whose final action routes through a worker thread or an existing helper process, unlike ExploitGym's setuid-catflag oracle which is more permissive/general; (2) the unanimity-across-3-rounds grading rule deliberately undercredits flaky exploits that succeed on some but not all randomization rounds, so reported numbers are a reliability-conditioned floor, not a raw success rate (the paper separately reports seed-to-seed reliability is only moderate at Tier 3, ~60-80%); (3) the private-frontier reference (Mythos Preview) is not publicly deployed/reproducible by other researchers -- it was accessed only under a collaboration agreement with Anthropic, so its results are a forward-looking capability signal rather than a claim about currently deployed risk; (4) the adaptive-coaching arm is explicitly characterized as a harness-sensitivity probe, not an optimized coaching strategy per model, so the reported effects (helping some models, hurting Gemini 3.1 Pro badly) should not be read as the ceiling of what coaching could achieve; (5) the corpus is limited to 41 V8 bugs (a single engine/codebase), all reported no earlier than 2024, which the paper argues avoids target-drift/staleness issues (via pinned Debian image digests, apt snapshots, and DEPS pinning) but also means findings may not generalize to other hardened targets without their own oracle design; (6) episodes are capped by turn count (300 turns) rather than wall-clock/cost, which the paper argues removes a provider-economics confound but also means the study cannot rule out that additional turns beyond 300 would let more public models cross the sandbox boundary, though the authors argue the roughly order-of-magnitude-per-tier cost growth and absence of budget-exhaustion clustering at the boundary is evidence against a pure budget artifact.

datasets_used: 41 V8 (Chromium JavaScript/WebAssembly engine) N-day bugs, all reported no earlier than 2024, spanning WebAssembly type-confusion bugs, JIT-compiler bugs (maglev, ignition, explicit-resource-management), and a historical-cohort subset; each bug bounty-eligible at $10,000 under Google's v8CTF
quantitative_results: Full evaluation matrix: 41 bugs x (9 models x 2 arms + 1 model x 1 CLI arm) x 3 seeds = 2,337 episodes
Mythos Preview (primary arm): Tier5=41/41, Tier4=37/41, Tier3=35/41, Tier2=21/41, pc_control=18/41, ace=18/41, mean cost $203.93/episode
Mythos Preview (nudged/coaching arm): Tier5=41, Tier4=38, Tier3=37, Tier2=27, pc_control=16, ace=16, mean cost $298.59/episode
Opus 4.7 (primary): Tier5=41, Tier4=24, Tier3=12, Tier2=0, ace=0, cost $29.56/ep
Sonnet 4.6 (primary): Tier5=41, Tier4=21, Tier3=10, Tier2=0, ace=0, cost $35.45/ep
Haiku 4.5 (primary): Tier5=40, Tier4=5, Tier3=0, Tier2=0, ace=0, cost $0.81/ep
GPT-5.5 (primary): Tier5=41, Tier4=27, Tier3=13, Tier2=2, pc_control=1, ace=0, cost $51.40/ep
GPT-5.5 (nudged): Tier5=41, Tier4=32, Tier3=22, Tier2=1, pc_control=0, ace=0, cost $66.86/ep
GPT-5.5 (Codex CLI arm): Tier5=41, Tier4=31, Tier3=20, Tier2=1, pc_control=1, ace=1 (only cell in the paper where a public model reaches ace), cost ~$10/ep
Gemini 3.1 Pro (primary): Tier5=40, Tier4=23, Tier3=16 (broadest Tier-3 spread on panel), Tier2=0, ace=0, cost $28.04/ep
Gemini 3.1 Pro (nudged): Tier5=29, Tier4=11, Tier3=8, Tier2=0 -- coaching sharply hurt this model, with ~3x increase in mid-episode API failures that terminated episodes early
GLM 5.1 (primary): Tier5=38, Tier4=13, Tier3=3, Tier2=0, ace=0, cost $6.49/ep
Kimi K2.6 (primary): Tier5=41, Tier4=16, Tier3=0, Tier2=0, ace=0, cost $5.41/ep; (nudged) Tier3 rises to 3
MiniMax M2.7 (primary): Tier5=40, Tier4=6, Tier3=0, Tier2=0, ace=0, cost $0.77/ep
ace reached in 19 of 2,337-episode-derived (model,bug) cells total: 18 are Mythos Preview under the primary arm; the 19th is GPT-5.5 under Codex CLI on v8-cve-2024-2887 (seed 1, turn 165, $17.80)
On v8-cve-2024-2887 under the primary arm, Mythos reaches ace on all 3 seeds at median 118 turns / $43; GPT-5.5 reaches every ladder flag except ace on seed 2
Mythos's successful ACE trajectory: coverage + engine primitives within ~first 40 turns, cage-bounded read/write by turn 60, full chain to ace between turn 110-200

Cited comparison baselines from prior benchmarks (not this paper's own results): CVE-Bench reports SOTA agents exploit up to 13% of web-app CVE tasks; Patch-to-PoC reports LLMs crash 56% of evaluated Linux kernel bugs via LLM-as-judge; CyberGym covers 1,507 vulnerabilities across 188 projects with crash-only success; ExploitGym packages 898 instances (userspace, V8, Linux kernel) with binary LLM-judged outcomes and reports ~8x turn-count spread across models under a fixed 2-hour wall-clock budget
Direct provider-API routing (vs. an OpenRouter-style gateway) achieved ~99% prompt-cache-hit rates vs. 0% for the routed alternative, halving per-cell cost with no change to capability scores
baselines_compared: Nine frontier models compared against each other as the panel: Anthropic Mythos Preview (private/non-public reference), Claude Opus 4.7, Claude Sonnet 4.6, Claude Haiku 4.5 (public), OpenAI GPT-5.5 (public), Google Gemini 3.1 Pro (public), Z.ai GLM 5.1 (public), Moonshot Kimi K2.6 (public), MiniMax M2.7 (public)

Prior benchmarks discussed as related work / methodological contrast rather than as baselines EXPLOITBENCH's numbers are directly compared against: BountyBench (40 web bounties, detect/exploit/patch as separate pass/fail tasks), CVE-Bench (web-app CVEs, fuzz-harness entrypoints, sanitizer oracle), CyberGym (1,507 vulnerabilities/188 projects, crash-reproduction success), Patch-to-PoC (Linux kernel, LLM-as-judge), ExploitGym (898 instances incl. V8, LLM-as-judge, setuid-catflag ACE oracle), ARVO (cited for environment-reproducibility/dependency-drift challenges)
